preFix.title: Roast potatoes
preFix.subtitle: Duck fat, par-boiled, shaken for crisp edges
preFix.excerpt: Floury potatoes par-boiled, shaken rough in the pan, then roasted in hot duck fat until deeply golden with shattered edges. The essential Sunday side.
preFix.sourceNotes:
Mrs Beeton, Book of Household Management (1861), Project Gutenberg #10136. Roasted potato entries.

Eliza Acton, Modern Cookery for Private Families (1845), Project Gutenberg. Potato cookery.

content.text: The best roast potatoes are built in two stages: a boil that softens the outside, and a roast in very hot fat that crisps it into a shattered shell. The shake in the colander after boiling is what creates the rough, starchy surface that crisps up in the oven.

items.name: Potato
items.prepNote: floury variety such as Maris Piper or King Edward, peeled and cut into large chunks

content.text: A wide, shallow roasting tin is better than a deep one. The potatoes need space to roast without steaming each other.
Put the potato chunks into a large saucepan, cover with cold salted water, and bring to the boil.
for 8 to 10 minutes, until the edges of the pieces are just starting to soften.
Drain thoroughly in a colander, then shake the colander vigorously a few times to rough up the surface of the potatoes. Leave to steam-dry for 5 minutes.
Heat the oven to 200 degrees C fan. Put the duck fat into the roasting tin and heat in the oven for 8 to 10 minutes until the fat is shimmering and almost smoking.
Carefully lower the potatoes into the hot fat, turning them so each piece is coated. Add the rosemary sprigs and garlic if using. Season with salt.
Roast for 45 to 50 minutes, turning once or twice, until deeply golden and crisp on all sides. Do not turn them more than twice; each turn breaks the forming crust.

items.fix: Heat the fat until shimmering before adding the potatoes. Give each piece room; if the tin is crowded, use two tins.
items.cause: The fat was not hot enough when the potatoes went in, or they were crowded in the tin.
items.symptom: Potatoes are golden on the outside but soft, not crisp

items.fix: Leave them untouched for at least 25 minutes before the first turn. They release naturally once a crust has formed.
items.cause: Turned too early before the crust has formed.
items.symptom: Potatoes are sticking to the tin

items.fix: Par-boil until the edges are visibly softening. Shake the colander firmly to build up a starchy, rough surface.
items.cause: The potatoes were not par-boiled long enough, or were not shaken well after draining.
items.symptom: The inside is fluffy but the outside is thin and pale

items.fix: Cut the potatoes into large chunks, at least 4 to 5 cm. Stop the boil as soon as the edges soften.
items.cause: Over-par-boiled so the flesh broke down, or the pieces were too small.
items.symptom: Potatoes are dry and dense inside

content.text: Goose fat gives an especially rich result for Christmas. Semolina or polenta dusted over the potatoes before they go into the fat adds extra crunch.

Roast potatoes have been alongside roast meat since the mid-nineteenth century. The par-boil-and-shake method became standard in post-war British cooking. It is now so embedded that most cooks learn it before they learn to boil an egg.
